title: Multi-seam project & task platform (store+migrations, API, worker, admin CLI)

security._note: Ticket #122, item 3: only the security MUSTs REACHABLE under this scenario's 'no external web framework' + bearer-token prompt are scored. Password hashing (a vetted KDF) and server-side per-user/ownership authorization (the idor surface) are reachable and expected. The other three — a schema-validation library import (inputValidation), httpOnly cookies (safeTokenStorage; bearer tokens live in the Authorization header, not cookies), and a rate-limit library import (authRateLimiting) — are unreachable under those constraints and were pinning the security spine at a floor no arm could clear, so they are excluded.
security._seams_note: Story #184 kept the auth/projects/tasks route contract intact across the multi-seam redesign, so both applicableMusts (and all six trap classes) still bind to the same surfaces.

description: The DECOMPOSITION value rung (Story #184; evolves the Epic #66/Story #78 large-capability rung). Cohorts through mandrel 2.12 showed the old seed — one cohesive project/task REST API — was a capability mandrel's default-single split policy is RIGHT to keep whole: the rung's own storyCountContract expected exactly 1 Story, so it measured a large single Story while charging epic-labelled ceremony, and its cost ratio was inflated by construction. The redesigned seed carries GENUINE architectural seams: four cooperating units (a shared store+migrations unit the others depend on, an HTTP API, a background report worker, and an admin CLI) with a real ordering edge (the store schema must exist before any consumer). /plan is expected to author N>1 Stories with at least one dependency edge; the machine-readable storyCountContract (mode multi-story, 3-5 Stories) makes a collapse back to one Story FAIL decomposition sanity rather than pass silently, and `routing: multi-story` drives the harness's sibling-Story discovery + `/deliver <ids>` path. 'epic' remains BENCHMARK terminology for the size of the delivered capability, not a mandrel ticket tier (Story #158). COST: a true epic cell is expensive by construction — this is deliberately an N=1-2 rung (`targetN` 2); do not scale replicates up to chase noise bands. The frozen suite verifies the COMPOSED system across all four seams over the app's observable boundaries (HTTP, plus `npm run admin`/`npm run migrate` executed in the delivered workspace via the harness-threaded workspacePath); the touch-2 change request (project sharing) amends exactly one seam (the API). Six planted defect classes are scored by SEPARATE adversarial trap-oracles the frozen suite is blind to: plaintext-password and hardcoded-secret (the token signing key inlined in source instead of read from the environment) as source-scanning regression sentinels, plus four BEHAVIOURAL probes that boot the delivered app — idor (id-addressed task reads/updates reached through routes the frozen suite does not sample), pagination-bounds (hostile ?page/?pageSize values), cascade-delete (tasks orphaned by a deleted project still reachable), and session-invalidation (a tampered bearer credential still authenticates). This redesign is a FROZEN-INSTRUMENT change: it ships behind a benchmarkVersion bump (issue #124 policy — a seed edit is by definition a benchmarkVersion bump), so its cells never mix with pre-redesign cohorts.

seed.title: Multi-user project & task platform: store+migrations, REST API, report worker, admin CLI
seed.prompt:
Build a multi-user project and task management platform in Node.js (ESM, no external web framework), delivered as one repository containing four cooperating units that all share a single persistent on-disk store (an embedded SQLite database file or an equivalent on-disk store — not in-memory).

**Unit 1 — the store and its migrations.** One unit owns the store's schema and is the only place schema changes live; the other three units depend on it. `npm run migrate` applies any pending schema migrations and is safe to run repeatedly. The API server also applies pending migrations when it boots, so a fresh checkout works straight away.

**Unit 2 — the JSON REST API server**, listening on the port given by the PORT environment variable (default 3000).

*Accounts* — users register with a username and password (POST /auth/register with a JSON body {"username": ..., "password": ...}, responding with the new user's id and username) and log in at POST /auth/login (same body shape), which responds with {"token": ...} — a bearer token the client sends in the Authorization header on every other request.

*Projects* — a signed-in user can create a project (POST /projects with {"name": ...}, responding with the created project: id, name, ownerId, createdAt), list the projects they own (GET /projects, responding with a JSON array), fetch one (GET /projects/:id), and delete one (DELETE /projects/:id) — deleting a project also removes the tasks in it.

*Tasks* — within one of their projects, a user can add a task (POST /projects/:projectId/tasks with {"title": ...}, responding with the created task: id, title, projectId, createdAt, and done: false), list tasks (GET /projects/:projectId/tasks — paginated as {"items": [...], "total": ..., "page": ..., "pageSize": ...} via ?page and ?pageSize query parameters, with an optional ?done=true|false filter), update one (PATCH /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId with {"title": optional, "done": optional}, responding with the updated task), and remove one (DELETE /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId).

*Report jobs* — POST /projects/:projectId/exports asks for a task report on one of the user's projects. Producing a report takes time, so this endpoint must respond immediately with a job record ({"id": ..., "status": ...}) and WITHOUT the report; the report is produced later by the worker (unit 3), never inside the request handler. The client polls GET /projects/:projectId/exports/:exportId; once the worker has finished, the job's status is "completed" and the job carries {"report": {"name": ..., "total": ..., "done": ...}} — the project's name, its task count, and how many of its tasks are done.

**Unit 3 — a background worker** that picks up queued report jobs from the shared store, produces each report, and marks the job "completed". `npm start` must bring up the WHOLE platform — the API server and the worker — so queued jobs complete without any extra command.

**Unit 4 — an admin CLI** for operators, run from the repository as `npm run admin -- <command>`:
- `npm run admin -- stats` prints a single JSON object {"users": ..., "projects": ..., "tasks": ...} counting what is currently in the store.
- `npm run admin -- deactivate-user <username>` deactivates the named account; a deactivated user can no longer log in.

This is a multi-user system, and the project, task, and export endpoints are for signed-in users. Handle bad or malformed requests sensibly — the server should never crash on one. All data must survive a server restart. Add npm "start", "migrate", and "admin" scripts.
acceptance: POST /auth/register with valid credentials responds 2xx with { id, username } and persists the user
POST /auth/register with a duplicate username, or with missing or empty fields, is rejected with a 4xx status
POST /auth/login with valid credentials responds 2xx with a bearer token
POST /auth/login with an unknown username or wrong password is rejected as unauthenticated (401/403)
Requests to a protected endpoint without a token, or with an invalid token, are rejected as unauthenticated (401/403)
POST /projects with a valid name responds 2xx with the created project (id, name, ownerId, createdAt)
POST /projects with an empty name is rejected with a 4xx status
GET /projects returns a JSON array containing only the authenticated user's own projects, never another user's projects
GET /projects/:id returns the matching project when owned by the authenticated user, and a 4xx (never the project) for an unknown id
GET /projects/:id for a project owned by a different user is denied (403/404) without leaking the project
DELETE /projects/:id removes the project and all its tasks (2xx); a subsequent GET no longer returns it
DELETE /projects/:id for a project owned by a different user is denied (403/404) and leaves the project intact
POST /projects/:projectId/tasks with a valid title responds 2xx with the task (id, title, projectId, createdAt, done: false)
POST /projects/:projectId/tasks with a missing or empty title is rejected with a 4xx status
POST /projects/:projectId/tasks against another user's project is denied (403/404)
GET /projects/:projectId/tasks returns a paginated response { items, total, page, pageSize } respecting ?page and ?pageSize
GET /projects/:projectId/tasks with a ?done=true or ?done=false filter returns only tasks matching that done value
PATCH /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId updates title and/or done and returns the updated task
DELETE /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId removes the task (2xx); the task list no longer includes it
POST /projects/:projectId/exports responds 2xx with a job record carrying an id and a status that is not yet "completed", without the finished report
GET /projects/:projectId/exports/:exportId reaches status "completed" within the polling window, with a report whose name and task counts (total, done) match the project
POST /projects/:projectId/exports against another user's project is denied (403/404), and one user's export job is not readable by another user
npm run admin -- stats prints a JSON object whose users, projects, and tasks counts reflect the data created over HTTP
After npm run admin -- deactivate-user <username>, POST /auth/login for that user is rejected (401/403) while other users still log in
npm run migrate exits 0, exits 0 again when re-run, and leaves existing data intact
A syntactically malformed JSON request body is rejected with a 4xx status and the server keeps serving subsequent requests (no crash, no silent accept)
Data for users, projects, tasks, and completed export jobs persists across a server restart

changeRequest.title: Share a project with another user with a role
changeRequest.prompt:
Project owners need to share projects with other users. Add POST /projects/:id/shares to the API server, taking a JSON body {"userId": ..., "role": "viewer"|"editor"}. Only a project's owner can share it. A user the project is shared with can then work with it according to their role: a viewer can view the project and its tasks but cannot change anything; an editor can also create, update, and delete its tasks.

Sharing must not weaken privacy: a user with no relationship to a project (neither its owner nor shared with it) still must not be able to see or touch it. Handle bad requests (an unknown user, an unrecognised role) sensibly.

This change is confined to the API unit and the store schema it reads — the worker and the admin CLI keep their existing behaviour.
